summary: Gruber argues that Apple will never acquire Adobe, dismantling the then-popular speculation with several clear reasons. He contends that Steve Jobs wants Apple to feel small and focused, and absorbing a $20 billion company with 7,000 employees would undermine that. Apple under Jobs only pursues best-of-breed products, and Flash — the most commonly cited reason for the acquisition — doesn't meet that standard. Gruber dismisses the defensive argument that Apple might buy Adobe to prevent Microsoft from doing so, noting Jobs plays offense, not defense. He concludes that Apple's real focus is mobility, and Adobe simply doesn't fit.
key_points: Jobs deliberately kept Apple feeling small and focused despite its size; acquiring a 7,000-person company would destroy that culture
Apple under Jobs only pursues best-of-breed technology, and Flash is ubiquitous but not good — the same reason iPhone uses Objective-C instead of Java
If Apple wanted Flash-like technology, they'd build their own superior version rather than buy someone else's
The defensive argument (buy Adobe before Microsoft does) doesn't hold because Jobs is an offensive thinker, and Microsoft could sink Mac apps without buying Adobe
Apple's strategic focus is mobility — iPhone, iPod, MacBook Air — and Adobe doesn't fit that direction at all
money_quotes: Steve Jobs wants Apple to feel like a small, focused company.
Flash is ubiquitous, but that doesn't make it good.
If Apple wanted to own a technology like Flash they'd build their own technically superior version and distribute it to Windows users with iTunes.
The decision-makers at Apple genuinely believe it to be decidedly better.
.Mac is .bad, but you wait and see if Apple doesn't knock it down and replace it with something reliable and more relevant and useful.
Steve Jobs is not a defensive thinker. Jobs plays offense.
It's not the pro market. It's mobility — iPhone, iPod, MacBook Air.
key_insight: Apple under Jobs would never acquire Adobe because Jobs only pursues best-of-breed technology he can tightly control, and his strategic focus on mobility makes Adobe's sprawling product portfolio irrelevant to Apple's direction.
